1:
"And the whole congregation of the children of Israel assembled together at Shiloh, and set up the tabernacle of the congregation there. And the land was subdued before them." The witnesses note the whole congregation gathering at Shiloh to set up the tabernacle, the land being subdued. The witnesses note that Gill notes the whole body of the people came thither from Gilgal, where the camp and tabernacle had been. The witnesses note that the land was subdued before them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the congregation assembled — at Shiloh; the set up the tabernacle — god's dwelling fixed; the the land subdued — before them; the God in their midst — this is my rest for ever (Ps. 132:14).

The witnesses agree this verse marks a great turning-point — the tabernacle, the dwelling of God, now fixed at Shiloh in the midst of the conquered land: "they... set up the tabernacle... there. And the land was subdued before them"; the moving of the sanctuary from Gilgal to Shiloh, a more central and settled place, shows that God now took up his rest among his people in their inheritance; the name Shiloh itself bespeaks rest and peace; it teaches that the LORD dwells in the midst of his people, that worship is the centre of their settled life, and that the conquered land found its true crown in the presence of God among them, "this is my rest for ever: here will I dwell" (Ps. 132:14), the tabernacle set up at Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse the tabernacle at Shiloh: The witnesses note the the congregation assembled: at Shiloh. The witnesses note the set up the tabernacle: God's dwelling fixed. The witnesses note the the land subdued: before them. The witnesses note the God in their midst: this is my rest for ever (Ps. 132:14). The expositors observe the the congregation assembled: at Shiloh. The expositors observe the set up the tabernacle: God's dwelling fixed. The expositors observe the the land subdued: before them. The expositors observe the God in their midst: this is my rest for ever (Ps. 132:14). The believer is taught to mark the the congregation assembled, the set up the tabernacle, the the land subdued, the God in their midst, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the congregation set up the tabernacle at Shiloh, the land subdued — God taking up his rest in the midst of his people, worship the centre of their settled life, this is my rest for ever, here will I dwell (Ps. 132:14).
2:
"And there remained among the children of Israel seven tribes, which had not yet received their inheritance." The witnesses note that seven tribes had not yet received their inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were Benjamin, Simeon, Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the seven tribes not yet provided for. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven tribes — remained; the had not received — their inheritance; the the land before them — ready but unclaimed; the the gift unenjoyed — all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21).

The witnesses agree that though Judah and Joseph were settled, "seven tribes... had not yet received their inheritance": the work of dividing was unfinished, and these tribes still wanted their portion though the land lay before them; the delay was not from God's withholding but from the people's own slowness, the inheritance ready but unclaimed; it teaches that God's gifts may be long unenjoyed through the slackness of his people, the promised portion waiting to be possessed; the believer too may have an inheritance in Christ not yet entered upon, "all things are yours" (1 Cor. 3:21) and yet not laid hold of, the seven tribes still without their portion.

So the witnesses make the verse seven tribes unsettled: The witnesses note the seven tribes: remained. The witnesses note the had not received: their inheritance. The witnesses note the the land before them: ready but unclaimed. The witnesses note the the gift unenjoyed: all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21). The expositors observe the seven tribes: remained. The expositors observe the had not received: their inheritance. The expositors observe the the land before them: ready but unclaimed. The expositors observe the the gift unenjoyed: all things are yours (1 Cor. 3:21). The believer is taught to mark the seven tribes, the had not received, the the land before them, the the gift unenjoyed, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that seven tribes had not yet received their inheritance — the gift ready but unclaimed through the people's slowness, God's portion long unenjoyed, all things theirs yet not laid hold of (1 Cor. 3:21).
3:
"And Joshua said unto the children of Israel, How long are ye slack to go to possess the land, which the LORD God of your fathers hath given you?" The witnesses note Joshua's reproof of the tribes' slackness in possessing the land God had given. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua said this to those of the seven tribes. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rebuke, How long are ye slack to possess the land the LORD hath given you? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the how long are ye slack — the reproof; the to possess the land — their own portion; the the LORD hath given — by promise; the against sloth — be not slothful, inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12).

The witnesses agree Joshua's rebuke strikes at the spiritual sloth that left God's gift unpossessed: "How long are ye slack to go to possess the land, which the LORD... hath given you?"; the land was theirs by promise and conquest, yet they delayed to take it, content to linger without their portion; the reproof teaches that it is a fault to be slow in laying hold of what God has freely given, the slackness of unbelief or ease robbing the people of their own; it warns the believer against spiritual sloth, to be diligent to possess all that grace has granted, "be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith... inherit the promises" (Heb. 6:12), Joshua rousing the slack to possess the land.

So the witnesses make the verse how long are ye slack: The witnesses note the how long are ye slack: the reproof. The witnesses note the to possess the land: their own portion. The witnesses note the the LORD hath given: by promise. The witnesses note the against sloth: be not slothful, inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12). The expositors observe the how long are ye slack: the reproof. The expositors observe the to possess the land: their own portion. The expositors observe the the LORD hath given: by promise. The expositors observe the against sloth: be not slothful, inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12). The believer is taught to mark the how long are ye slack, the to possess the land, the the LORD hath given, the against sloth, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Joshua reproved the tribes' slackness to possess the land God had given — the fault of being slow to lay hold of grace's gift, the believer warned against spiritual sloth, be not slothful but inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12).
4:
"Give out from among you three men for each tribe: and I will send them, and they shall rise, and go through the land, and describe it according to the inheritance of them; and they shall come again to me." The witnesses note Joshua's plan — three men from each tribe to survey and describe the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes three men for each of the seven tribes, twenty-one in all; some think out of all the tribes. The witnesses note that they should go through the land and describe it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three men per tribe — to be chosen; the go through the land — and survey it; the describe it — according to the inheritance; the diligence with faith — all things decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).

The witnesses agree Joshua set in motion an orderly survey of the unallotted land, choosing men to go through it and "describe it according to the inheritance": the careful, deliberate method shows that the possessing of God's gift is to be done with diligence and order, not by haste or chance; the appointed men would view the whole and bring back a true account; it teaches that the people of God are to use prudent means in entering upon what he gives, joining diligence to faith, surveying and ordering the inheritance, "let all things be done decently and in order" (1 Cor. 14:40), three men sent from each tribe to describe the land.

So the witnesses make the verse survey the land: The witnesses note the three men per tribe: to be chosen. The witnesses note the go through the land: and survey it. The witnesses note the describe it: according to the inheritance. The witnesses note the diligence with faith: all things decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The expositors observe the three men per tribe: to be chosen. The expositors observe the go through the land: and survey it. The expositors observe the describe it: according to the inheritance. The expositors observe the diligence with faith: all things decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40). The believer is taught to mark the three men per tribe, the go through the land, the describe it, the diligence with faith, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Joshua appointed men to survey and describe the land — the possessing of God's gift done with diligence and order, joining prudent means to faith, all things decently and in order (1 Cor. 14:40).
5:
"And they shall divide it into seven parts: Judah shall abide in their coast on the south, and the house of Joseph shall abide in their coasts on the north." The witnesses note the land to be divided into seven parts for the seven tribes, Judah and Joseph already settled. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was to be divided into seven parts according to the seven tribes not yet settled. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Judah abiding on the south, Joseph on the north. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the divide it into seven — for the seven tribes; the Judah on the south — already settled; the Joseph on the north — in their place; the a place for each — many mansions in my Father's house (John 14:2).

The witnesses agree the remaining land was to be parted into seven, with Judah to the south and Joseph to the north already in their places: "they shall divide it into seven parts"; the bounds of those already settled framed the portions of the rest, each tribe to have its appointed share; the orderly partition shows God's care that every tribe receive its due, none crowded out or forgotten; it teaches that the LORD provides a place for each of his people, dividing to every one his portion within the whole, "in my Father's house are many mansions" (John 14:2), the land divided into seven parts for the seven tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse seven parts: The witnesses note the divide it into seven: for the seven tribes. The witnesses note the Judah on the south: already settled. The witnesses note the Joseph on the north: in their place. The witnesses note the a place for each: many mansions in my Father's house (John 14:2). The expositors observe the divide it into seven: for the seven tribes. The expositors observe the Judah on the south: already settled. The expositors observe the Joseph on the north: in their place. The expositors observe the a place for each: many mansions in my Father's house (John 14:2). The believer is taught to mark the divide it into seven, the Judah on the south, the Joseph on the north, the a place for each, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the land was to be divided into seven parts, Judah and Joseph framing the rest — God's care that every tribe receive its due, a place for each within the whole, many mansions in the Father's house (John 14:2).
6:
"Ye shall therefore describe the land into seven parts, and bring the description hither to me, that I may cast lots for you here before the LORD our God." The witnesses note the survey to be brought to Joshua, who would cast lots before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were first to describe the land, then divide it into seven parts, and bring the description. The witnesses note that Joshua would cast lots before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the describe the land — man's diligence; the bring it to me — to Joshua; the cast lots — the disposing God's; the before the LORD — the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33).

The witnesses agree the dividing joined human diligence to divine appointment — the men would survey and describe, but the final allotting was by "lots... before the LORD our God": the survey was man's part, the disposing God's; the casting of lots before the LORD referred the whole outcome to him, that each tribe might own its portion as from his hand; it teaches that the people of God use means and labour, yet commit the result to him, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Prov. 16:33), the description brought to Joshua and the lots cast before God.

So the witnesses make the verse lots before the LORD: The witnesses note the describe the land: man's diligence. The witnesses note the bring it to me: to Joshua. The witnesses note the cast lots: the disposing God's. The witnesses note the before the LORD: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The expositors observe the describe the land: man's diligence. The expositors observe the bring it to me: to Joshua. The expositors observe the cast lots: the disposing God's. The expositors observe the before the LORD: the disposing is of the LORD (Prov. 16:33). The believer is taught to mark the describe the land, the bring it to me, the cast lots, the before the LORD, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the survey was brought to Joshua to cast lots before the LORD — human diligence joined to divine appointment, the outcome committed to God, the whole disposing of the lot his (Prov. 16:33).
7:
"But the Levites have no part among you; for the priesthood of the LORD is their inheritance: and Gad, and Reuben, and half the tribe of Manasseh, have received their inheritance beyond Jordan..." The witnesses note the Levites having no land, the priesthood being their inheritance, and the eastern tribes already settled. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Levites needed send no men to measure land, having no part, which is why the land was divided into seven parts only. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the priesthood of the LORD their inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Levites no part — no land; the the priesthood — their inheritance; the Gad, Reuben, half-Manasseh — settled east; the God their portion — the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24).

The witnesses agree the chapter again marks the special portion of Levi — not land, but the priesthood: "the priesthood of the LORD is their inheritance"; the tribe given to God's service had for their heritage the holy office and the LORD himself, a portion above all earthly division; the eastern tribes too were already provided beyond Jordan; it teaches that those set apart to serve God find their inheritance in him and his service, the ministry itself their portion, a richer heritage than fields, "the LORD is my portion... I will hope in him" (Lam. 3:24), the priesthood the inheritance of Levi.

So the witnesses make the verse the priesthood their inheritance: The witnesses note the the Levites no part: no land. The witnesses note the the priesthood: their inheritance. The witnesses note the Gad, Reuben, half-Manasseh: settled east. The witnesses note the God their portion: the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24). The expositors observe the the Levites no part: no land. The expositors observe the the priesthood: their inheritance. The expositors observe the Gad, Reuben, half-Manasseh: settled east. The expositors observe the God their portion: the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24). The believer is taught to mark the the Levites no part, the the priesthood, the Gad, Reuben, half-Manasseh, the God their portion, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the Levites had no land, the priesthood being their inheritance — those set apart to serve God finding their portion in him and his service, the ministry a richer heritage than fields, the LORD my portion (Lam. 3:24).
8:
"And the men arose, and went away: and Joshua charged them that went to describe the land, saying, Go and walk through the land, and describe it, and come again to me..." The witnesses note the men setting out at Joshua's charge to survey and describe the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the seven tribes took Joshua's advice, chose three men each, and presented them to him for their commission. The witnesses note that they were to walk through the land and describe it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the men arose — and went; the walk through the land — the survey; the describe it — their charge; the prompt obedience — do it with thy might (Eccl. 9:10).

The witnesses agree the appointed men obeyed and set out on their charge: "the men arose, and went away... Go and walk through the land, and describe it"; the prompt obedience of the surveyors, taking up the task at Joshua's word, shows the readiness that should attend God's work; their walking through the whole land to describe it was a faithful discharge of their commission; it teaches that the servants of God are to rise and do the work appointed them without delay, faithful in the task given, "whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might" (Eccl. 9:10), the men arising to walk through and describe the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the men arose: The witnesses note the the men arose: and went. The witnesses note the walk through the land: the survey. The witnesses note the describe it: their charge. The witnesses note the prompt obedience: do it with thy might (Eccl. 9:10). The expositors observe the the men arose: and went. The expositors observe the walk through the land: the survey. The expositors observe the describe it: their charge. The expositors observe the prompt obedience: do it with thy might (Eccl. 9:10). The believer is taught to mark the the men arose, the walk through the land, the describe it, the prompt obedience, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the men arose at Joshua's charge to walk through and describe the land — the readiness that should attend God's work, faithful in the task given, doing it with all their might (Eccl. 9:10).
9:
"And the men went and passed through the land, and described it by cities into seven parts in a book, and came again to Joshua to the host at Shiloh." The witnesses note the men passing through the land unmolested, describing it in a book, and returning to Shiloh. The witnesses note that Gill notes they passed through undisturbed, the fear of Israel still on the inhabitants and the providence of God restraining them. The witnesses note that they described it by cities in a book and returned to Joshua. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the passed through the land — unmolested; the described it by cities — in a book; the seven parts — the orderly record; the God's protection — the angel encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7).

The witnesses agree the survey was carried through in safety under God's protecting providence: "the men went and passed through the land... and came again to Joshua... at Shiloh"; though the land still held its old inhabitants, the dread of Israel and the restraining hand of God kept the surveyors from harm; the careful describing "by cities... in a book" shows the orderly record made of the inheritance; it teaches that God protects his servants in their appointed work, restraining their enemies and bringing them safely through, "the angel of the LORD encampeth round about them that fear him, and delivereth them" (Ps. 34:7), the men passing safely through and returning to Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse described in a book: The witnesses note the passed through the land: unmolested. The witnesses note the described it by cities: in a book. The witnesses note the seven parts: the orderly record. The witnesses note the God's protection: the angel encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7). The expositors observe the passed through the land: unmolested. The expositors observe the described it by cities: in a book. The expositors observe the seven parts: the orderly record. The expositors observe the God's protection: the angel encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7). The believer is taught to mark the passed through the land, the described it by cities, the seven parts, the God's protection, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that the men passed safely through the land and described it in a book — God protecting his servants in their work, restraining their enemies, the angel of the LORD encamping round about them (Ps. 34:7).
10:
"And Joshua cast lots for them in Shiloh before the LORD: and there Joshua divided the land unto the children of Israel according to their divisions." The witnesses note Joshua casting lots at Shiloh before the LORD, dividing the land to the seven tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua cast lots for the seven tribes at Shiloh, as he had for the two and a half at Gilgal. The witnesses note that he divided the land according to their divisions. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cast lots — at Shiloh; the before the LORD — at his presence; the divided the land — to the tribes; the all referred to God — acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6).

The witnesses agree the final allotment was made solemnly "in Shiloh before the LORD," at the place of his presence: the casting of lots before the tabernacle referred the whole division to God, that each tribe might receive its portion as his own assignment; the doing of it before the LORD sanctified the act and stilled all dispute; it teaches that the affairs of God's people are best ordered in his presence and committed to his disposal, the inheritance divided not by man's will but by God's lot before him, "in all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths" (Prov. 3:6), Joshua casting lots at Shiloh before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse lots at Shiloh: The witnesses note the cast lots: at Shiloh. The witnesses note the before the LORD: at his presence. The witnesses note the divided the land: to the tribes. The witnesses note the all referred to God: acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6). The expositors observe the cast lots: at Shiloh. The expositors observe the before the LORD: at his presence. The expositors observe the divided the land: to the tribes. The expositors observe the all referred to God: acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6). The believer is taught to mark the cast lots, the before the LORD, the divided the land, the all referred to God, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Joshua cast lots at Shiloh before the LORD, dividing the land — the inheritance referred wholly to God's disposal, each portion his assignment, in all thy ways acknowledge him and he shall direct thy paths (Prov. 3:6).
11:
"And the lot of the tribe of the children of Benjamin came up according to their families: and the coast of their lot came forth between the children of Judah and the children of Joseph." The witnesses note Benjamin's lot coming up first, set between Judah and Joseph. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the first of the seven lots; a small lot, "little Benjamin," but the land very pleasant and fruitful. The witnesses note that it lay between Judah and Joseph. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Benjamin's lot — came up first; the between Judah and Joseph — a choice place; the little Benjamin — small but pleasant; the the least honoured — god chose the things which are not (1 Cor. 1:28-29).

The witnesses agree Benjamin's lot, the first of the seven, fell in a choice place "between the children of Judah and the children of Joseph": though small — "little Benjamin" — the portion was pleasant and fruitful, and set between the two greatest tribes; the placing of the beloved youngest son's tribe in so honoured a position, near where the temple would stand, shows God's favour to the small; it teaches that the LORD honours the least and sets them in places of blessing, the small tribe given a goodly and central portion, "God hath chosen... the things which are not... that no flesh should glory" (1 Cor. 1:28-29), little Benjamin's lot between Judah and Joseph.

So the witnesses make the verse Benjamin's lot: The witnesses note the Benjamin's lot: came up first. The witnesses note the between Judah and Joseph: a choice place. The witnesses note the little Benjamin: small but pleasant. The witnesses note the the least honoured: God chose the things which are not (1 Cor. 1:28-29). The expositors observe the Benjamin's lot: came up first. The expositors observe the between Judah and Joseph: a choice place. The expositors observe the little Benjamin: small but pleasant. The expositors observe the the least honoured: God chose the things which are not (1 Cor. 1:28-29). The believer is taught to mark the Benjamin's lot, the between Judah and Joseph, the little Benjamin, the the least honoured, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's lot came up first, between Judah and Joseph — God honouring the small, the least tribe given a goodly and central portion, choosing the things which are not that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:28-29).
12:
"And their border on the north side was from Jordan; and the border went up to the side of Jericho on the north side, and went up through the mountains westward..." The witnesses note Benjamin's northern border, running from Jordan by Jericho through the mountains. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jordan was the eastern boundary, and the northern border ran from east to west, described like the lot of Joseph. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the northern border of Benjamin. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Jordan — the eastern bound; the by Jericho — northward; the through the mountains — westward; the the least cared for — not a sparrow falls without your Father (Matt. 10:29).

The witnesses agree Benjamin's northern border is now traced from the Jordan by Jericho westward through the hills: the careful bounding of even the smallest tribe's portion shows the same exactness shown to the great; God's reckoning overlooked none, marking the borders of little Benjamin as precisely as those of Judah; it teaches that the LORD's care is equal toward the least of his people, their portion as surely fixed and bounded as any, none neglected in his appointment, "are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall... without your Father" (Matt. 10:29), Benjamin's border traced from Jordan westward.

So the witnesses make the verse the northern border: The witnesses note the from Jordan: the eastern bound. The witnesses note the by Jericho: northward. The witnesses note the through the mountains: westward. The witnesses note the the least cared for: not a sparrow falls without your Father (Matt. 10:29). The expositors observe the from Jordan: the eastern bound. The expositors observe the by Jericho: northward. The expositors observe the through the mountains: westward. The expositors observe the the least cared for: not a sparrow falls without your Father (Matt. 10:29). The believer is taught to mark the from Jordan, the by Jericho, the through the mountains, the the least cared for, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's northern border ran from Jordan by Jericho westward — the same exactness shown the smallest tribe as the great, God's care equal toward the least, not a sparrow falling without the Father (Matt. 10:29).
13:
"And the border went over from thence toward Luz, to the side of Luz, which is Bethel, southward..." The witnesses note Benjamin's border passing by Luz, which is Bethel — the place of Jacob's vision. The witnesses note that Gill notes from Beth-aven the western border began and went on to Luz. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Luz, which is Bethel, southward. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the toward Luz — the western border; the which is Bethel — the house of God; the Jacob's vision — the gate of heaven; the the true Bethel — angels ascending on the Son of man (John 1:51).

The witnesses agree Benjamin's border ran by "Luz, which is Bethel" — the very place where Jacob saw the ladder reaching to heaven and called it "the house of God" and "the gate of heaven" (Gen. 28:17-19): the storied site of the patriarch's vision fell within the border of little Benjamin; the city where God met Jacob became part of the tribe's heritage; it teaches that God settles his people in places hallowed by his past dealings, the scenes of his grace bound up with their inheritance; Bethel, the house of God, points to the true meeting-place of heaven and earth in Christ, on whom the angels ascend and descend (John 1:51), Benjamin's border passing by Bethel.

So the witnesses make the verse by Bethel: The witnesses note the toward Luz: the western border. The witnesses note the which is Bethel: the house of God. The witnesses note the Jacob's vision: the gate of heaven. The witnesses note the the true Bethel: angels ascending on the Son of man (John 1:51). The expositors observe the toward Luz: the western border. The expositors observe the which is Bethel: the house of God. The expositors observe the Jacob's vision: the gate of heaven. The expositors observe the the true Bethel: angels ascending on the Son of man (John 1:51). The believer is taught to mark the toward Luz, the which is Bethel, the Jacob's vision, the the true Bethel, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's border passed by Bethel, the place of Jacob's vision — God settling his people in places hallowed by his grace, Bethel pointing to Christ the true meeting of heaven and earth, the angels ascending on the Son of man (John 1:51).
14:
"And the border was drawn thence... and the goings out thereof were at Kirjathbaal, which is Kirjathjearim, a city of the children of Judah: this was the west quarter." The witnesses note Benjamin's western border ending at Kirjath-jearim, once Kirjath-baal. The witnesses note that Gill notes the border drawn from Beth-horon. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the Israelites changed the name Kirjath-baal, to blot out the remembrance of Baal. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the border drawn — from Beth-horon; the Kirjath-baal — renamed Kirjath-jearim; the the name changed — to blot out Baal; the idolatry rooted out — take away the names of Baalim (Hos. 2:17).

The witnesses agree the western border ran out to Kirjath-jearim, formerly "Kirjath-baal," whose very name was changed to erase the memory of the idol: Wesley notes "the Israelites changed the name, to blot out the remembrance of Baal"; the renaming of the city was a deliberate putting-away of idolatry from the inheritance; it teaches that God's people are to root out every trace of false worship, even the names that honour idols, that the land be wholly the LORD's, "take away the names of Baalim out of her mouth" (Hos. 2:17); the changed name marks the heritage cleansed for God, Benjamin's border ending at the city once Baal's.

So the witnesses make the verse the name of Baal blotted: The witnesses note the the border drawn: from Beth-horon. The witnesses note the Kirjath-baal: renamed Kirjath-jearim. The witnesses note the the name changed: to blot out Baal. The witnesses note the idolatry rooted out: take away the names of Baalim (Hos. 2:17). The expositors observe the the border drawn: from Beth-horon. The expositors observe the Kirjath-baal: renamed Kirjath-jearim. The expositors observe the the name changed: to blot out Baal. The expositors observe the idolatry rooted out: take away the names of Baalim (Hos. 2:17). The believer is taught to mark the the border drawn, the Kirjath-baal, the the name changed, the idolatry rooted out, and to go up and possess his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's border ended at Kirjath-jearim, once Kirjath-baal, its name changed to blot out the idol — God's people rooting out every trace of false worship, taking away the names of Baalim out of her mouth (Hos. 2:17).
15:
"And the south quarter was from the end of Kirjathjearim, and the border went out on the west, and went out to the well of waters of Nephtoah:" The witnesses note Benjamin's southern border, running from Kirjath-jearim to the well of Nephtoah. The witnesses note that Gill notes the south quarter began where the western quarter ended, at Kirjath-jearim. The witnesses note that it went out to the well of waters of Nephtoah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the south quarter — from Kirjath-jearim; the the border west — then south; the the well of waters — of Nephtoah; the a portion watered — draw water from the wells of salvation (Isa. 12:3).

The witnesses agree the southern border of Benjamin is now traced from Kirjath-jearim to "the well of waters of Nephtoah": the bound ran by a place of springs, the waters that refreshed the land marking the heritage; the careful drawing of even this border shows the exactness of God's gift, each line fixed by his appointment; the well among the bounds may remind the believer of the springs of living water that flow through the inheritance of God's people; it teaches that the LORD gives his own a portion watered and supplied, the well within the border a token of his provision, "ye shall draw water out of the wells of salvation" (Isa. 12:3), Benjamin's south border by the well of Nephtoah.

So the witnesses make the verse the well of Nephtoah: The witnesses note the the south quarter: from Kirjath-jearim. The witnesses note the the border west: then south. The witnesses note the the well of waters: of Nephtoah. The witnesses note the a portion watered: draw water from the wells of salvation (Isa. 12:3). The expositors observe the the south quarter: from Kirjath-jearim. The expositors observe the the border west: then south. The expositors observe the the well of waters: of Nephtoah. The expositors observe the a portion watered: draw water from the wells of salvation (Isa. 12:3). The believer is taught to mark the the south quarter, the the border west, the the well of waters, the a portion watered, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's southern border ran by the well of Nephtoah — the exactness of God's gift, the heritage watered and supplied, a token of his provision, drawing water out of the wells of salvation (Isa. 12:3).
16:
"And the border came down to the end of the mountain that lieth before the valley of the son of Hinnom... in the valley of the giants on the north..." The witnesses note the border descending by the valley of Hinnom and the valley of the giants. The witnesses note that Gill notes here the measure was from west to east, where in Judah's border it went up. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the border by the valley of the son of Hinnom. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the border came down — to the mountain; the the valley of Hinnom — later defiled; the the valley of the giants — northward; the a byword of judgment — the wicked turned into hell (Ps. 9:17).

The witnesses agree the border ran by "the valley of the son of Hinnom" — the place that became infamous as the scene of child-sacrifice to Molech, and whose name (Gehenna) came to stand for hell itself (2 Chr. 28:3): the very valley of later abomination lay within the bounds of the inheritance; the heritage of God's people held places that would be defiled by sin and become bywords of judgment; it teaches that even the land God gives may be polluted by his people's apostasy, and warns against the abominations that turned a border-valley into a figure of perdition, "the wicked shall be turned into hell" (Ps. 9:17), the valley of Hinnom on Benjamin's border.

So the witnesses make the verse the valley of Hinnom: The witnesses note the the border came down: to the mountain. The witnesses note the the valley of Hinnom: later defiled. The witnesses note the the valley of the giants: northward. The witnesses note the a byword of judgment: the wicked turned into hell (Ps. 9:17). The expositors observe the the border came down: to the mountain. The expositors observe the the valley of Hinnom: later defiled. The expositors observe the the valley of the giants: northward. The expositors observe the a byword of judgment: the wicked turned into hell (Ps. 9:17). The believer is taught to mark the the border came down, the the valley of Hinnom, the the valley of the giants, the a byword of judgment, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the border ran by the valley of Hinnom — the place of later abomination within the heritage, a warning that the land may be polluted by apostasy, the valley become a figure of perdition, the wicked turned into hell (Ps. 9:17).
17:
"And was drawn from the north, and went forth to Enshemesh, and went forth toward Geliloth... and descended to the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben," The witnesses note the border by En-shemesh, "the fountain of the sun," a witness to the old idolatry. The witnesses note that Gill notes the border turned northward, looking from west to east. The witnesses note that Clarke notes En-shemesh, "the fountain of the sun," a proof of the idolatrous nature of the ancient inhabitants. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the drawn from the north — the border turned; the En-shemesh — the fountain of the sun; the the old idolatry — sun-worship; the against worshipping the creature — lest thou worship the sun (Deut. 4:19).

The witnesses agree the border passed by "Enshemesh" — "the fountain of the sun," whose name, Clarke notes, is "a proof of the idolatrous nature of the ancient inhabitants," who worshipped the sun: the heritage of God's people bore in its very place-names the marks of the false worship that had filled the land; the sun-spring stood as a witness to the idolatry from which Israel was to keep itself; it teaches that the land was cleansed of peoples who served the creature rather than the Creator, and warns God's own against the like, "lest thou lift up thine eyes unto heaven, and when thou seest the sun... shouldest be driven to worship them" (Deut. 4:19), En-shemesh on Benjamin's border.

So the witnesses make the verse En-shemesh the sun-spring: The witnesses note the drawn from the north: the border turned. The witnesses note the En-shemesh: the fountain of the sun. The witnesses note the the old idolatry: sun-worship. The witnesses note the against worshipping the creature: lest thou worship the sun (Deut. 4:19). The expositors observe the drawn from the north: the border turned. The expositors observe the En-shemesh: the fountain of the sun. The expositors observe the the old idolatry: sun-worship. The expositors observe the against worshipping the creature: lest thou worship the sun (Deut. 4:19). The believer is taught to mark the drawn from the north, the En-shemesh, the the old idolatry, the against worshipping the creature, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the border passed En-shemesh, "the fountain of the sun," a witness to the old idolatry — the land cleansed of those who served the creature, God's people warned against worshipping the sun (Deut. 4:19).
18:
"And passed along toward the side over against Arabah northward, and went down unto Arabah:" The witnesses note the border passing by Arabah, the same as Beth-arabah. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the same with Beth-arabah, so called in the Greek version. The witnesses note that the border went down unto Arabah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the toward Arabah — the desert plain; the northward — the border ran; the down unto Arabah — the lowland; the a varied inheritance — the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness (Ps. 24:1).

The witnesses agree the border ran on by "Arabah," the desert plain, the same as Beth-arabah named before: the bound reached even into the wilderness reaches of the land, the heritage taking in the lowland plain as well as the hills; the tracing of the border through such varied country shows the fullness of the portion God assigned; it teaches that the LORD gives his people a complete and varied inheritance, plain and hill alike, no part of his gift omitted, the whole compass of the land theirs by his appointment, "the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof" (Ps. 24:1), the border passing down to Arabah.

So the witnesses make the verse down to Arabah: The witnesses note the toward Arabah: the desert plain. The witnesses note the northward: the border ran. The witnesses note the down unto Arabah: the lowland. The witnesses note the a varied inheritance: the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness (Ps. 24:1). The expositors observe the toward Arabah: the desert plain. The expositors observe the northward: the border ran. The expositors observe the down unto Arabah: the lowland. The expositors observe the a varied inheritance: the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness (Ps. 24:1). The believer is taught to mark the toward Arabah, the northward, the down unto Arabah, the a varied inheritance, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the border passed down to Arabah — the heritage taking in plain and hill alike, the LORD giving a complete and varied inheritance, the earth and its fulness his (Ps. 24:1).
19:
"And the border passed along to the side of Bethhoglah northward: and the outgoings of the border were at the north bay of the salt sea at the south end of Jordan: this was the south coast." The witnesses note the border ending at the north bay of the Salt Sea, where Jordan empties. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beth-hoglah was included in the lot of Benjamin. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the north bay of the Salt Sea. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Beth-hoglah — northward; the the north bay — of the Salt Sea; the the south end of Jordan — where it ends; the sure bounds — thou hast set a bound (Ps. 104:9).

The witnesses agree Benjamin's southern coast ran out "at the north bay of the salt sea at the south end of Jordan": the border reached to the very waters of the Dead Sea, where Jordan ends its course; the heritage was bounded by the great natural marks of the land, fixed and unmistakable; the drawing of the border to the sea shows the certainty and definiteness of God's gift, set within sure and visible bounds; it teaches that the LORD assigns to his people a portion clearly marked and secure, fenced by his appointment, "thou hast set a bound that they may not pass over" (Ps. 104:9), Benjamin's south coast at the Salt Sea.

So the witnesses make the verse the Salt Sea: The witnesses note the Beth-hoglah: northward. The witnesses note the the north bay: of the Salt Sea. The witnesses note the the south end of Jordan: where it ends. The witnesses note the sure bounds: thou hast set a bound (Ps. 104:9). The expositors observe the Beth-hoglah: northward. The expositors observe the the north bay: of the Salt Sea. The expositors observe the the south end of Jordan: where it ends. The expositors observe the sure bounds: thou hast set a bound (Ps. 104:9). The believer is taught to mark the Beth-hoglah, the the north bay, the the south end of Jordan, the sure bounds, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Benjamin's south coast ran out at the bay of the Salt Sea — the heritage bounded by sure and visible marks, God's gift clearly fixed and secure, who has set a bound that may not be passed (Ps. 104:9).
20:
"And Jordan was the border of it on the east side. This was the inheritance of the children of Benjamin, by the coasts thereof round about, according to their families." The witnesses note Jordan as Benjamin's eastern border, completing the compass of its inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Benjamin had Jordan on the east, Dan on the west, Judah on the south, and Joseph on the north. The witnesses note that this was the inheritance of Benjamin, round about, by their families. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jordan on the east — the border; the the inheritance of Benjamin — round about; the among the tribes — in the heart of the land; the a goodly heritage — i have a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:5).

The witnesses agree the description of Benjamin closes with Jordan on the east and the full compass of the portion set down: "this was the inheritance of the children of Benjamin... round about, according to their families"; hemmed by Judah, Joseph, Dan, and the Jordan, little Benjamin had a goodly portion in the very heart of the land; the rounded account marks the completeness and security of the gift; it teaches that God gives his people a portion entire and well-bounded, set in its place among the rest, the inheritance of the small tribe as sure as any, "the LORD is the portion of mine inheritance... I have a goodly heritage" (Ps. 16:5), Benjamin's inheritance complete round about.

So the witnesses make the verse Benjamin's inheritance: The witnesses note the Jordan on the east: the border. The witnesses note the the inheritance of Benjamin: round about. The witnesses note the among the tribes: in the heart of the land. The witnesses note the a goodly heritage: I have a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:5). The expositors observe the Jordan on the east: the border. The expositors observe the the inheritance of Benjamin: round about. The expositors observe the among the tribes: in the heart of the land. The expositors observe the a goodly heritage: I have a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:5). The believer is taught to mark the Jordan on the east, the the inheritance of Benjamin, the among the tribes, the a goodly heritage, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Jordan bounded Benjamin on the east, completing its inheritance round about — God giving his people a portion entire and well-bounded, set in its place, a goodly heritage (Ps. 16:5).
21:
"Now the cities of the tribe of the children of Benjamin according to their families were Jericho, and Bethhoglah, and the valley of Keziz," The witnesses note the cities of Benjamin, beginning with Jericho, the first conquest. The witnesses note that Gill introduces the cities of Benjamin. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Jericho, Beth-hoglah, and the valley of Keziz as the first towns. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cities of Benjamin — listed; the Jericho — the first conquest; the Beth-hoglah — and Keziz; the the field of wonders a home — brought to the border of his sanctuary (Ps. 78:54).

The witnesses agree the roll of Benjamin's cities opens with Jericho, the first city taken in the land, whose walls fell by faith (Josh. 6:20): the first-fruits of the conquest, the city of the great miracle, now stands as an inheritance of little Benjamin; the place where God showed his power at the outset became a settled possession of his people; it teaches that the scenes of God's mighty works are given to his own for a heritage, the city of faith's first victory now a peaceable dwelling, the LORD turning the field of his wonders into the home of his people, "he brought them to the border of his sanctuary" (Ps. 78:54), Jericho first among Benjamin's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Jericho first: The witnesses note the the cities of Benjamin: listed. The witnesses note the Jericho: the first conquest. The witnesses note the Beth-hoglah: and Keziz. The witnesses note the the field of wonders a home: brought to the border of his sanctuary (Ps. 78:54). The expositors observe the the cities of Benjamin: listed. The expositors observe the Jericho: the first conquest. The expositors observe the Beth-hoglah: and Keziz. The expositors observe the the field of wonders a home: brought to the border of his sanctuary (Ps. 78:54). The believer is taught to mark the the cities of Benjamin, the Jericho, the Beth-hoglah, the the field of wonders a home, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the cities of Benjamin open with Jericho, the first conquest — the scene of faith's first victory now a peaceable inheritance, God turning the field of his wonders into the home of his people (Ps. 78:54).
22:
"And Betharabah, and Zemaraim, and Bethel," The witnesses note Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, and Bethel among Benjamin's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Beth-arabah, of which he speaks elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Zemaraim, probably the ruins of es Sumrah, and Bethel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Beth-arabah — and Zemaraim; the Bethel — the house of God; the Jacob's ladder — and later apostasy; the keep his house holy — the gate of heaven (Gen. 28:17).

The witnesses agree the list names Bethel, "the house of God," the place where Jacob saw the heavenly ladder and God renewed his covenant (Gen. 28:19): the storied sanctuary-town fell within Benjamin's portion, though sadly it became in later days a seat of Jeroboam's calf-worship; the city of holy memory and of later apostasy alike was part of the heritage; it teaches that the places most hallowed by God's grace may be most defiled by man's sin, and bids God's people keep the house of God truly his, "this is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven" (Gen. 28:17), Bethel among Benjamin's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Bethel the house of God: The witnesses note the Beth-arabah: and Zemaraim. The witnesses note the Bethel: the house of God. The witnesses note the Jacob's ladder: and later apostasy. The witnesses note the keep his house holy: the gate of heaven (Gen. 28:17). The expositors observe the Beth-arabah: and Zemaraim. The expositors observe the Bethel: the house of God. The expositors observe the Jacob's ladder: and later apostasy. The expositors observe the keep his house holy: the gate of heaven (Gen. 28:17). The believer is taught to mark the Beth-arabah, the Bethel, the Jacob's ladder, the keep his house holy, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Bethel, the house of God, is named among Benjamin's cities — the place of holy memory and later apostasy, the most hallowed may be most defiled, the house of God to be kept truly his, the gate of heaven (Gen. 28:17).
23:
"And Avim, and Parah, and Ophrah," The witnesses note Avim, Parah, and Ophrah among the cities of Benjamin. The witnesses note that Gill notes of the first two of these we read nowhere else. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Avvim (i.e., ruins) is unknown, Phara preserved in the ruins of Fara. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Avim — i.e., ruins; the Parah — scarcely traced; the Ophrah — little known; the men's works decay — the word of our God shall stand for ever (Isa. 40:8).

The witnesses agree these towns are little known — "Avim" means "ruins," and the others are scarcely traced elsewhere; yet they were real cities of Benjamin's heritage, given and kept by God though forgotten by men: the very name "ruins" reminds us how the works of men decay, while the gift of God endures in his record; it teaches that the cities of men crumble and pass, but the LORD's appointment stands, his people's portion sure though the stones fall, "the grass withereth, the flower fadeth: but the word of our God shall stand for ever" (Isa. 40:8), Avim and its fellows among Benjamin's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse the ruins among them: The witnesses note the Avim: i.e., ruins. The witnesses note the Parah: scarcely traced. The witnesses note the Ophrah: little known. The witnesses note the men's works decay: the word of our God shall stand for ever (Isa. 40:8). The expositors observe the Avim: i.e., ruins. The expositors observe the Parah: scarcely traced. The expositors observe the Ophrah: little known. The expositors observe the men's works decay: the word of our God shall stand for ever (Isa. 40:8). The believer is taught to mark the Avim, the Parah, the Ophrah, the men's works decay, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that these obscure cities, one named "ruins," are part of Benjamin's heritage — the works of men decaying while God's appointment stands, his people's portion sure, the word of our God standing for ever (Isa. 40:8).
24:
"And Chepharhaammonai, and Ophni, and Gaba; twelve cities with their villages:" The witnesses note the close of the first group — twelve cities, including Gaba (Geba). The witnesses note that Gill notes of the first two of these no mention is made elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Chephar-haammonai, Ophni, and Gaba, twelve cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Chephar-haammonai — and Ophni; the Gaba — later fortified; the twelve cities — exactly numbered; the with their villages — none uncounted (Ps. 147:4).

The witnesses agree the first group of Benjamin's cities closes with "twelve cities with their villages," including Geba, a town of note later fortified and made a bound of Judah's kingdom (2 Kgs 23:8): the exact reckoning of twelve, each with its villages, marks the completeness of the record; the careful numbering shows that God leaves none of his gifts uncounted; it teaches that the LORD's provision is precise and full, every city and hamlet of the heritage numbered before him, the believer's portion measured and sure, "he telleth the number of the stars; he calleth them all by their names" (Ps. 147:4) — the God who numbers the stars numbering Benjamin's cities, twelve with their villages.

So the witnesses make the verse twelve cities: The witnesses note the Chephar-haammonai: and Ophni. The witnesses note the Gaba: later fortified. The witnesses note the twelve cities: exactly numbered. The witnesses note the with their villages: none uncounted (Ps. 147:4). The expositors observe the Chephar-haammonai: and Ophni. The expositors observe the Gaba: later fortified. The expositors observe the twelve cities: exactly numbered. The expositors observe the with their villages: none uncounted (Ps. 147:4). The believer is taught to mark the Chephar-haammonai, the Gaba, the twelve cities, the with their villages, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the first group closed with twelve cities exactly numbered — God's provision precise and full, every city and hamlet numbered before him, he who telleth the number of the stars calling them by name (Ps. 147:4).
25:
"Gibeon, and Ramah, and Beeroth," The witnesses note Gibeon, the city of the Gibeonites who deceived Joshua, among Benjamin's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gibeon is the place from whence the Gibeonites came, who deceived Joshua; a village four miles from Bethel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Gibeon, Ramah, and Beeroth. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Gibeon — the deceivers' city; the spared for the oath — and made servants; the later a high place — where Solomon sacrificed; the strangers brought near — joined to the LORD to serve him (Isa. 56:6).

The witnesses agree the list names Gibeon, the city of those crafty Gibeonites who by guile won a league with Israel (Josh. 9:3): the town of the deceivers, spared for the oath's sake and made servants of God's house, now stands within Benjamin's heritage; Gibeon would later be a high place where the tabernacle stood and where Solomon sacrificed and received wisdom (1 Kgs 3:4-5); it teaches that God's mercy can bring even the once-deceiving stranger into the place of his worship and blessing, the city of the Gibeonites become a seat of sacrifice, "strangers... joined to the LORD, to serve him" (Isa. 56:6), Gibeon among Benjamin's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Gibeon of the Gibeonites: The witnesses note the Gibeon: the deceivers' city. The witnesses note the spared for the oath: and made servants. The witnesses note the later a high place: where Solomon sacrificed. The witnesses note the strangers brought near: joined to the LORD to serve him (Isa. 56:6). The expositors observe the Gibeon: the deceivers' city. The expositors observe the spared for the oath: and made servants. The expositors observe the later a high place: where Solomon sacrificed. The expositors observe the strangers brought near: joined to the LORD to serve him (Isa. 56:6). The believer is taught to mark the Gibeon, the spared for the oath, the later a high place, the strangers brought near, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Gibeon, city of the Gibeonites who deceived Joshua, is named among Benjamin's cities — God's mercy bringing the once-deceiving stranger into the place of worship, strangers joined to the LORD to serve him (Isa. 56:6).
26:
"And Mizpeh, and Chephirah, and Mozah," The witnesses note Mizpeh, a place often named in Scripture, among Benjamin's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes frequent mention is made of Mizpeh in Scripture, about eight miles from Gibeah, near Ramah. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Mizpeh, Chephirah, and Mozah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Mizpeh — the watchtower; the Samuel gathered Israel — to pray; the the victory given — ebenezer raised; the a place of help — hitherto hath the LORD helped us (1 Sam. 7:12).

The witnesses agree the list names Mizpeh ("the watchtower"), a place of great note in Israel's later history: there Samuel gathered the people to repent and pray, and there God thundered against the Philistines and gave the victory, so that Samuel set up the stone Ebenezer, "hitherto hath the LORD helped us" (1 Sam. 7:5-12); the town of Benjamin's heritage became a hallowed place of national prayer and deliverance; it teaches that God appoints places where his people seek him and find help, the watchtower-city a scene of repentance and rescue; it bids the believer raise his own Ebenezer of thankful remembrance, "hitherto hath the LORD helped us," Mizpeh among Benjamin's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Mizpeh of prayer: The witnesses note the Mizpeh: the watchtower. The witnesses note the Samuel gathered Israel: to pray. The witnesses note the the victory given: Ebenezer raised. The witnesses note the a place of help: hitherto hath the LORD helped us (1 Sam. 7:12). The expositors observe the Mizpeh: the watchtower. The expositors observe the Samuel gathered Israel: to pray. The expositors observe the the victory given: Ebenezer raised. The expositors observe the a place of help: hitherto hath the LORD helped us (1 Sam. 7:12). The believer is taught to mark the Mizpeh, the Samuel gathered Israel, the the victory given, the a place of help, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Mizpeh, the later scene of Samuel's gathering and Israel's rescue, is named among Benjamin's cities — God appointing places where his people seek him and find help, raising the Ebenezer, hitherto hath the LORD helped us (1 Sam. 7:12).
27:
"And Rekem, and Irpeel, and Taralah," The witnesses note Rekem, Irpeel, and Taralah among Benjamin's cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes of these cities there is no mention made elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Rekem, Irpeel, and Taralah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Rekem — nowhere else named; the Irpeel — obscure; the Taralah — lost to history; the known to God — the LORD knoweth them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19).

The witnesses agree these towns, "Rekem, and Irpeel, and Taralah," are nowhere else named in Scripture, yet were truly part of Benjamin's portion: though men can give no account of them, they were known to God and given by him; the obscurity of these places to us, while they stood sure in the inheritance, witnesses that God's gifts do not depend on man's remembrance; it teaches that the LORD values and keeps what the world forgets, the least-known of his provisions as surely his gift as the greatest, the hidden cities of Benjamin numbered in his record though lost to history, "the LORD knoweth them that are his" (2 Tim. 2:19), Rekem and its fellows among Benjamin's towns.

So the witnesses make the verse the unknown cities: The witnesses note the Rekem: nowhere else named. The witnesses note the Irpeel: obscure. The witnesses note the Taralah: lost to history. The witnesses note the known to God: the LORD knoweth them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19). The expositors observe the Rekem: nowhere else named. The expositors observe the Irpeel: obscure. The expositors observe the Taralah: lost to history. The expositors observe the known to God: the LORD knoweth them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19). The believer is taught to mark the Rekem, the Irpeel, the Taralah, the known to God, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that these unknown cities were part of Benjamin's portion — God's gifts not depending on man's remembrance, the least-known as surely his gift, the LORD knowing them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19).
28:
"And Zelah, Eleph, and Jebusi, which is Jerusalem, Gibeath, and Kirjath; fourteen cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the children of Benjamin according to their families." The witnesses note Jerusalem and Gibeah among Benjamin's cities, closing the inheritance of fourteen cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Zelah was the burying-place of Saul and his family. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Zelah, the burying-place of Saul, Jonathan, and the family of Kish, and Jebusi, which is Jerusalem. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Zelah — saul's burial; the Jebusi, which is Jerusalem — the future city of God; the Gibeath — gibeah of Saul; the great things in small places — the LORD hath chosen Zion (Ps. 132:13).

The witnesses agree the roll closes with names of great weight — "Jebusi, which is Jerusalem," and "Gibeath" (Gibeah of Saul), with Zelah where Saul and Jonathan were buried (2 Sam. 21:14): the border of little Benjamin held both the future city of God and the city of Israel's first king; Jerusalem, then still the Jebusite stronghold, would become the seat of David's throne and the place of the temple, the city of the great King; from Benjamin too would come Saul, and long after, Saul of Tarsus, the apostle Paul; it teaches that God lays up great things in small places, the tribe of the least holding the city of his choice, "the LORD hath chosen Zion... for his habitation" (Ps. 132:13), Jerusalem among Benjamin's fourteen cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Jerusalem and Gibeah: The witnesses note the Zelah: Saul's burial. The witnesses note the Jebusi, which is Jerusalem: the future city of God. The witnesses note the Gibeath: Gibeah of Saul. The witnesses note the great things in small places: the LORD hath chosen Zion (Ps. 132:13). The expositors observe the Zelah: Saul's burial. The expositors observe the Jebusi, which is Jerusalem: the future city of God. The expositors observe the Gibeath: Gibeah of Saul. The expositors observe the great things in small places: the LORD hath chosen Zion (Ps. 132:13). The believer is taught to mark the Zelah, the Jebusi, which is Jerusalem, the Gibeath, the great things in small places, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the inheritance closed with Jerusalem and Gibeah among fourteen cities — little Benjamin holding the future city of God and the city of the first king, God laying up great things in small places, the LORD choosing Zion for his habitation (Ps. 132:13).
